1: 'The plans (ma'arĕkê) of the heart belong to man, but the answer (ma'anēh) of the tongue is from the LORD' — the verse turns on a contrast between human intention and divine determination of what actually comes out in speech. Ma'arĕkê = arrangements/orderings; ma'anēh = the actual answer/response. YHWH controls not just big history but the specific words that leave the mouth, which Proverbs regards as the most revealing indicator of a person's inner state.
2: Kol-darkê-'îš zak bĕ'ênāyw = 'All the ways of a man are pure in his own eyes' — radical self-assessment blindness. Tōkēn rûḥôt YHWH = 'but the LORD weighs/measures spirits.' Tākan (weigh/measure) is the precision-metrological verb used for calibrating scales — YHWH applies exact measurement to what humans cannot objectively assess in themselves.
3: Gōl 'el-YHWH ma'ăśeḵā = 'Roll your works to the LORD' — gāll (roll) is used for rolling away a stone or off-loading a burden; the visual image is of handing the whole weight of one's enterprises to YHWH. Wĕyikkōnû maḥšĕbōteḵā = 'and your plans will be established' — the MKT 'Commit your work' captures the intent but loses the rolling/burden-transfer image.
4: Kōl pā'al YHWH lamma'anēhû = 'The LORD has made everything for its purpose/answer' — ma'anēhû (its answer/purpose) echoes ma'anēh in v1: everything has its fitting response in the divine order. Wĕgam-rāšā' lĕyôm rā'āh = 'even the wicked for the day of calamity' — divine sovereignty extends over human wickedness itself, which serves its appointed function in YHWH's purposes without YHWH being the author of the wickedness.
5: Tô'ăbat YHWH kol-gĕbah-lēb = 'An abomination to the LORD is every proud-of-heart person' — gābah (be high/exalted) applied to the heart describes arrogance. Yād lĕyād = 'hand to hand' = a certain/sure oath gesture; the proud will certainly not go unpunished.
6: Bĕḥesed we'ĕmet yĕkuppar 'āwōn = 'By steadfast love and faithfulness (ḥesed we'emet) iniquity is atoned for/covered' — the covenant-pair terms here function as the moral rather than cultic means of atonement: the person of covenant-loyalty finds their sin covered. Yĕsūr mērā' = 'turns away from evil' — the fear of YHWH is the practical instrument of moral departure from evil.
7: 'When a man's ways please the LORD, he makes even his enemies be at peace with him' — yašlîm (make peace) — even hostile third parties become instruments of divine blessing when a person's life is aligned with YHWH's ways. This is the positive application of divine sovereignty: YHWH controls the dispositions of enemies.
8: A better-than saying: 'Better a little with righteousness than great revenues with injustice' (bĕṣĕdāqāh). The principle of qualitative over quantitative measurement runs through Proverbs; ṣĕdāqāh (righteousness/right-relationship) modifies the value of what is possessed.
9: Lēb 'ādām yĕḥaššēb darkô = 'The heart of a man plans his way' — the verb ḥāšab is the word for careful calculation/devising; wĕYHWH yākîn ṣa'ădô = 'but the LORD establishes his step.' Ṣa'ăd (step/stride) is the individual footfall — YHWH's providential control is not merely over large trajectories but over each specific step. The contrast of human planning with divine directing is the governing theme of Proverbs 16.
10: Qesem 'al-śiptê-melek = 'Divination/oracle is on the lips of the king' — qesem normally refers to prohibited divination (Deuteronomy 18:10), but here it is used positively for the quasi-oracular authority of the king's judicial pronouncements. The royal mouth speaks with binding authority; bĕmišpāṭ lō' yim'al pîw = 'his mouth should not err in judgment.'
11: Peles ûmō'zĕnê mišpāṭ lYHWH = 'A just balance and scales belong to the LORD' — YHWH is the ultimate standard of all measurement. The divine ownership of measuring instruments means that commercial dishonesty is not merely a social wrong but a theological offense. Kol-'abnê-kis = 'all the stones of the bag' = all the calibration weights.
12: 'An abomination to kings to do evil' — kî-bĕṣĕdāqāh yikkôn kissē' = 'for by righteousness the throne is established.' The throne's stability is a function of the righteousness of its occupant; this is the standard prophetic critique (Amos, Isaiah, Jeremiah) applied to the wisdom context: power without righteousness is self-undermining.
13: Śiptê-ṣedeq reṣôn mĕlākîm = 'Lips of righteousness are the delight of kings' — reṣôn (delight/favor) describes what the wise king values in counsel. Wĕ'ōhēb dibēr yĕšārîm = 'and one who speaks straight/upright things he loves.' The king who values honest counsel is functionally wise; the king who does not (see Proverbs 14:35; Proverbs 19:12) is a danger.
14: Za'ap-melek mal'ăkê māwet = 'The wrath of the king is messengers of death' — the plural mal'ăkê (messengers) figures the king's anger as an agency that sends out death-agents. Wĕ'îš ḥākām yĕkappĕrennāh = 'but a wise man will appease/atone for it.' Kāpar (atone/cover) applied to pacifying the king's anger — wisdom is the instrument that deflects lethal power.
15: 'ôr-pĕnê-melek ḥayyîm = 'In the light of the king's face is life' — the king's approving look gives life; ûrĕṣônô kĕ'āb malkôš = 'and his favor is like a cloud of spring rain.' The malkôš (spring rain, late/latter rain) is the rain that causes the grain to mature in the final weeks before harvest — the most life-giving of rains. Royal favor is that precious and that productive.
16: 'How much better to get wisdom than gold' (qĕnôh ḥokmāh mah-ṭôb mizzāhāb) — the comparative wisdom saying uses the commercial vocabulary of v8 (qānāh = acquire/buy). The parallel 'and to get understanding is to be chosen rather than silver' reinforces the value-comparison: wisdom beats both metals.
17: Mĕsillat yĕšārîm sûr mērā' = 'The highway of the upright is turning away from evil' — mĕsillāh is the paved royal road, not a footpath. The deliberate road-avoidance of evil is the upright person's characteristic movement. Šōmēr napšô nōṣēr darkô = 'the one who guards his soul keeps his way.'
18: Lifnê-šeber gā'ôn = 'Before destruction is pride' — the famous verse. Gā'ôn (pride/majesty) can be positive (divine majesty) or negative (human arrogance); here clearly the latter. Gōbah rûaḥ lifnê kāšel = 'and a haughty spirit before stumbling/falling.' The MKT 'Pride goes before destruction, and a haughty spirit before a fall' reproduces the standard English idiom derived from this verse; the Hebrew gā'ôn is more intense than 'pride' — it denotes presumptuous self-exaltation.
19: 'Better to be lowly (šĕpal-rûaḥ, low of spirit) with the poor than to divide spoil with the proud' — the antithesis between true humility (voluntarily taken low position with those of low status) and triumphalist gain-sharing with the arrogant. The socioeconomic dimension is deliberate: wisdom chooses the social bottom over the prideful top.
20: Maśkîl 'al-dābār = 'The one who attends wisely to a matter' (or 'who handles a word skillfully') yimṣā' ṭôb = 'will find good.' Ûbôṭēaḥ baYHWH = 'and blessed is the one who trusts in the LORD.' The two lines create an implied equation: skillful attention to affairs and trust in YHWH belong together; neither is separable from the other in Proverbs' wisdom theology.
21: Ḥăkam-lēb yiqqārē' nābôn = 'The wise of heart will be called discerning' — nābôn (discerning) is the social recognition of inner wisdom. Ûmeteq śĕpātayim yôsîp leqaḥ = 'and sweetness of lips increases learning/reception' — the communicative dimension of wisdom: not only possessing understanding but conveying it with meteq (sweetness/pleasantness) that makes it receivable.
22: Mĕqôr ḥayyîm śĕkel bĕ'ālāyw = 'A fountain of life is insight to its possessor' — the same fountain-of-life image as Proverbs 13:14; Proverbs 14:27 — wisdom as the source of life. Ûmûsar 'ĕwîlîm 'iwwelet = 'but the discipline of fools is folly' — discipline applied to the fool only produces more folly; the fool turns even correction into an expression of his nature.
23: Lēb ḥākām yaśkîl pîhû = 'The heart of the wise makes his mouth wise/teach skillfully' — the internal-external connection: a wise heart produces wise speech. Wĕ'al-śĕpātāyw yōsîp leqaḥ = 'and on his lips it adds learning/persuasive power.' The wise person's words carry more weight because they arise from genuine understanding rather than mere rhetorical skill.
24: Nōpet ṣûpîm 'imrê nō'am = 'Pleasant words are like honeycomb' — nōpet ṣûpîm is dripping/flowing honeycomb (the purest, finest honey). Marpe' lā'etsem = 'healing to the bones' — the physical metaphor for the restorative power of well-chosen words. The bones as the deepest bodily structure suggest that good speech heals at the most fundamental level.
25: Verbatim repetition of Proverbs 14:12: 'There is a way that seems right to a man, but its end is the way to death' (derek yāšār lipnê-'îš wĕ'aḥărîtāh darkê-māwet). The repetition of the saying is intentional — it belongs to the center of Proverbs 16's meditation on divine sovereignty (vv1,9,33 frame the chapter) and human self-deception. The way that 'seems right' is the alternative to YHWH-ordered steps (v9).
26: Nepeš 'āmēl 'āmĕlāh-lô = 'The soul/appetite of the laborer labors for him' — the worker is driven by his own nepeš (appetite/self). Kî-'āpap 'ālāyw pîhû = 'for his mouth urges/presses him' — hunger is the motor of labor; the bodily need drives the work. This is neutral observation, not critique: physical need is part of the created order that motivates human industry.
27: 'Îš bĕliyya'al kōreh rā'āh = 'A man of worthlessness digs evil' — kārar (dig) suggests deliberate labor to produce wickedness; evil doesn't happen accidentally for such a person, it is excavated. Wĕ'al-śĕpātāyw kĕ'ēš ṣōrābet = 'and on his lips is like scorching fire' — the speech of the worthless person burns those it touches.
28: 'Îš tahpukôt yĕšallēaḥ mādon = 'A man of perversities/distortions sends out strife' — tahpukôt (perversities) describes fundamental distortion of the moral order. Wĕnirgan maphrid allûp = 'and a whisperer separates close friends' — nirgan (whisperer/gossip) does maximal damage in the most intimate relationships; allûp = a close friend or intimate companion.
29: 'Îš ḥāmās yĕpatteh rē'ēhû = 'A violent man entices/seduces his neighbor' — pātāh (entice/seduce) is the word used for seduction in sexual contexts; the violent person uses persuasion before force. Wĕhôlîkô bĕderek lō'-ṭôb = 'and leads him in a way that is not good' — Proverbs' characteristic understatement for spiritual ruin.
30: 'One who winks with his eyes ('ōṣeh 'ênāyw) to devise perverse things, one who purses his lips (qōrēṣ śĕpātāyw)' — the non-verbal communication of malicious intent. These are the same body-language indicators as Proverbs 6:13-14: crooked dealings are signaled through eyes and lips before they are enacted in action. Kille' rā'āh = 'finishes/completes evil.'
31: Wĕśêbāh 'ăṭeret tipʾāret = 'Grey/white hair is a crown of glory' — śêbāh (grey hair/old age) as a crown is unusual; typically crowns are given by wisdom (Proverbs 4:9). Here old age itself is the crown, but only bĕderek ṣĕdāqāh timāṣē' = 'when found in the way of righteousness.' Long life that has been righteous is the crown; long wicked life is not crowned.
32: 'Erek 'appayim miggibôr = 'Slow to anger is better than a warrior' — 'erek 'appayim (long of nose/anger = patient/slow to anger) is the divine attribute of Exodus 34:6 applied to the human sphere. Ûmōšēl bĕrûḥô milōkēd 'îr = 'and the one who rules his spirit is better than one who conquers a city' — the most demanding conquest is internal. The military vocabulary applied to self-mastery is deliberately ironic.
33: Haggôrāl yûṭal bĕḥêq = 'The lot is cast into the lap' — the mechanism of lot-casting (Urim/Thummim; casting lots for decisions) is described; ûmēYHWH kol-mišpāṭô = 'but from the LORD is its every decision/judgment.' What appears to be chance is actually divine determination. This closes the divine-sovereignty inclusio of Proverbs 16 (vv1,9,33): YHWH governs the tongue (v1), the step (v9), and even the lot (v33).
